title: Kenton R. Miller Award
focus: Details of the Kenton R. Miller Award and the IUCN World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA).
rationale: This section covers a specific international award, its constituting body (WCPA), and a recent Indian recipient, making it a prime candidate for objective prelims questions.
sectionExcerpt: Kaziranga Director becomes first Indian to receive IUCN’s Kenton R. Miller Award for innovation in national parks. Constituted by: World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA), one of six technical commissions of IUCN. Established in 2006 for Innovation in National Parks and Protected Area Sustainability. Honors individuals who show innovation and leadership in conserving national parks and protected areas. Presented every two years by the IUCN-WCPA.
evidenceBullets: Kaziranga Director is the first Indian to receive the Kenton R. Miller Award.
The award is constituted by the World Commission on Protected Areas (WCPA), one of IUCN's six technical commissions.
Established in 2006, it honors innovation and leadership in conserving national parks and protected areas.
The award is presented every two years by the IUCN-WCPA.

title: IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC)
focus: Establishment, role, and recent Indian leadership appointment to the IUCN SSC.
rationale: The SSC is a major IUCN body, and the election of an Indian conservationist as its Chair is highly relevant for UPSC current affairs.
sectionExcerpt: Established in 1949, it is a science-based network of more than 10,500 volunteer experts. India’s renowned wildlife conservationist, Vivek Menon, has been elected as the new Chair of the IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC) for the 2025-2029. Role: To provide information to IUCN on biodiversity conservation, the inherent value of species, their role in ecosystem health and functioning, etc.
evidenceBullets: The IUCN Species Survival Commission (SSC) was established in 1949 as a science-based network of volunteer experts.
Indian wildlife conservationist Vivek Menon was elected as the new Chair of the IUCN SSC for 2025-2029.
The SSC provides information to IUCN on biodiversity conservation, species value, and ecosystem health.

title: IUCN World Heritage Outlook 4 Report
focus: Findings of the IUCN World Heritage Outlook 4 report specifically regarding Indian natural heritage sites.
rationale: Reports and indices are high-yield for UPSC. The specific categorization of Indian National Parks and the Western Ghats provides excellent factual anchors for statement-based questions.
sectionExcerpt: It categorises Western Ghats and 2 national parks (NP) in India – Assam’s Manas NP and West Bengal’s Sundarbans NP as being of “significant concern”. Other findings: Only Khangchendzonga National Park remained ‘Good’ in Conservation Outlook Rating. Sundarbans National Park: Witnessed Deterioration in its conservation outlook status from ‘Good with some Concerns’ to ‘Significant Concerns’ from 2020-2025.
evidenceBullets: The report categorizes the Western Ghats, Manas NP (Assam), and Sundarbans NP (West Bengal) as being of 'significant concern'.
Khangchendzonga National Park is the only Indian site that remained 'Good' in the Conservation Outlook Rating.
Sundarbans National Park's conservation outlook deteriorated from 'Good with some Concerns' to 'Significant Concerns' between 2020 and 2025.

title: Green Status Assessment for the Lion
focus: IUCN Green Status for the Lion, Asiatic Lion conservation profile, and morphological differences between Asiatic and African Lions.
rationale: Species profiles, especially for flagship species like the Asiatic Lion, and comparative biology (Asiatic vs. African) are classic UPSC question formats.
sectionExcerpt: IUCN releases First Green Status Assessment for the Lion (Panthera leo). Lion’s Green Status: ‘Largely depleted’. Extinct Regions: North Africa and Southwest Asia. Asiatic Lion Profile: WPA, 1972: Schedule I; CITES: Appendix I; IUCN Status: VU (Vulnerable). Difference between Asiatic and African Lions: Asiatic Lion has sparser and shorter mane (ears visible), a distinctive longitudinal skin fold along the belly, and is slightly smaller than African lions.
evidenceBullets: The First Green Status Assessment for the Lion categorizes its status as 'Largely depleted'.
The Asiatic Lion is listed as Vulnerable (VU) on the IUCN Red List, Schedule I of WPA 1972, and Appendix I of CITES.
Asiatic lions have a distinctive longitudinal skin fold along the belly, which is usually absent in African lions.
Male Asiatic lions have sparser and shorter manes compared to African lions, making their ears almost always visible.
Asiatic lions are generally smaller in body size than African lions.
